sentences.text: My father came back and he said that they ordered to deliver all-- old wounds, et cetera.
And my father said he overheard one of the Gestapo men from Jaslo, from headquarters, tell the Gestapo men from our town who were there-- Schmatsler, [?
Stangl, ?] [?
Vandeveer, ?] I know all their names.
I knew them all-- Becker-- tell them, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That means knock a few off tomorrow.
You know.
So we collected everything we could-- stockings, gloves, little pieces of fur anywheres.
You know.
And whether the stockings had holes in it didn't make any difference.
Everything had to be delivered.
And we delivered it.
And everyone delivered [? theirs. ?] In spite of it, a few people were shot that day, the following day with the excuse being that they caught them, say, with a little fur under the glove.
Or they caught them with something else.
I'm not so sure they caught them with any of it.
But this was the excuse.
So there were a number of people killed that day.
Now, that was the second episode of killing.
Whereas in between, nothing had happened.
So that was December, 1941.
And then it calmed down again.
Then came 1942.
And I remember around February in 1942 came to our store an individual, a big husky guy, without a arm band, you know, Star of David arm band.
And he said he worked at the air base, at our air base, for a company called Ascania, which was a company that was building fortifications near us.
And he worked for that company.
And he used to buy glass from us for the company.
So he came in, and he sat down.
And he was telling us that he was Jewish.
You know.
We didn't know.
He didn't wear an arm band.
That meant risking, you know, being shot.
And he said he was from Boynton, which is Bytom.
Boynton was Germany now-- I mean then.
He was Jewish.
And he had heard from reliable authority that by the end of the year, there would be no Jews alive anymore.
How he knew it, of course, now we know today, you know, that the Wannsee Conference at which place it was decided to kill all the Jews had taken place a few months earlier.
How we knew it, I don't know.
But he worked for the Germans there.
And he said, why don't we find a hiding place, because he would like to join us.
And I remember my father and my mother-- we talked about it.
And we didn't know is he Jewish or is he a spy.
You know.
We afraid.
That's number one.
How can you trust him?
Number two, who could imagine that you can kill millions of people?
It didn't make sense.
I mean, we have seen sporadic killing here and there.
But how-- how they kill millions?
No one believed it.
No one believed it.
So about a few months later, the order came to build a ghetto.
Now, all Jews living outside would have to move into the ghetto, including us.
We would have to go into the ghetto.
They took a small street, the Franciscan Street, [?
Francizkansa, ?] and blocked it off on either side, put the gate in, boarded up the windows to the outside.
And all Jews were to move in there.
And they put a Polish policemen and a Jewish policeman at the gate.
And you couldn't leave-- go and come without a special permit if you worked for the Germans.
We had one, because [?
Vandeveer ?] was Obersturmfuhrer [?
Vanderveer. ?] I remember his signature even on mine, you know, said that Alexander [INAUDIBLE] was, you know, can move in and out, you know, at will.
And so on.
So we all had that.
So we could leave, come, and go, and so on.
No, no, no.
Only myself, my father, my brother, and, you know, my one-year younger brother and my half cousin, you know, Heller, Moshe Heller.
So we were going in and out.
And we would, you know, scramble some food and so on.
For example, Friday, I would go there to a place to buy  a fish, you know, or buy a goose.
You know.
I would give the guy glass.
And he would give me the goose-- things of that  sort.
And we again survived.
Then came the order.
And it was either July or August.
I-- I can't remember that exactly.
It was hot.
The order came that the Jews-- all Jews have to show up at the cattle marketplace-- cattle marketplace near the train station.
One morning at such and such an hour, everybody-- the sick, the blind, the lame, made no difference-- children.
They had to be brought out by stretchers or wheelchairs.
Everybody has to show up there in the morning with a satchel of some food for three days, supposedly.
You know.
And whoever isn't going to show up is going to be shot on the spot.
Then they postponed it.
But the Jews had to pay a certain amount of money for it, and they postponed it  a few days.
Then they postponed it a second time, again, for monies.
They wanted so much money.
So the Jews scrambled together whatever they had-- if they had diamond rings, gold, and [? sold-- ?] sold on the black market to deliver these monies to the Gestapo.
When I think about it nowadays, I personally think that they didn't have the transportation.
You see, they had it arranged for that day.
But apparently, the transportation didn't come along.
So they said, now, if you'll pay us so much money, we will, you know, rescind the order.
So they rescinded it for a few days.
Then again two times they rescinded it.
Finally, we all had to show up.
Now, the evening before, we wanted to know what's going to be.
So I and my half cousin took a few plates of glass as if going to work and went to the Gestapo headquarters.
They now had their living place was in a villa a little bit away from where we were.
We walked over there.
The maid that used to be-- used to work for them-- used to cook for them-- we knew very well because she was a  maid to a Jewish neighbor of ours.
So her name was [?
Hela. ?] So my cousin and myself went there.
Hela ?] opens the door.
And we told [?
Hela ?] that we wanted to talk to Schmatsler, the head of the Gestapo.
And so she went in.
And I remember I peeked in through the door.
And there was a whole bunch them sitting eating and drinking, you know, including Gestapo not from our town.
They sat at the very long table.
Schmatsler come in-- comes out with his food still in this mouth.
And he immediately sensed what we were there for.
And he said, glasermeister, you are going to stay like this.
We wanted to know what it's going to be.
He said, you are going to stay.
So we went back.
We didn't trust him.
You know.
So what we did is we hid my sister and my little brother and my mother-- we hid them in the attic.
And my cousin hid their parents.
And we hid a number of people, you know, that we knew that can survive.
You know, that they are-- they are at risk, at greater risk than we were.
And we went out to the cattle market.

At the cattle market, there were all the Jews from our town plus many Jews that were resettled from areas in the West that the Germans took over and annexed as part of Germany from Warthegau from Litzmannstadt, which was [?
Luck. ?] There were many in our town, because they have to be resettled.
So the-- the ghetto, the Jews, the numbers grew.
Also the Jews from the little towns and the villages, from the shtetles, surrounding it all had to leave everything there and come in-- into our town.
See, what they're trying-- what they were trying to do is conglomerate them in one spot so they are easy to be handled rather than have to go from village to village to get the Jews.
And there were many Jews in the villages.
If you saw the movie you know, this is how they were.
They owned a tavern or they owned a little pension, [NON-ENGLISH].
Or they had a cow and a few chickens.
And they would sell milk, kosher milk, et cetera.
So in the villages, there were Jews and in the tiny towns.
And they all had to come in.
So we now, you know, exploded with people.
Everybody had to show up there.
So we went out there.
To describe to you  the commotion is impossible.
The place was surrounded by guards, mostly.
Ukrainian guardsmen and SS men, the entire place.
Many people from the town came out to watch-- non-Jewish people.
And there, we all stood and the Gestapo, not from-- not from our town but from other towns were there, with their whips whipping people here, there, continually.
The commotion went on something horrible.
But our Gestapo people were also there.
But they were not doing any hitting.
They were just standing by there.
As you stood there, I tell many people, you know, like for example, when you saw the movie.
And many of the non-Jewish Poles, gentiles, you know, were standing there and were very happy to see the Jews being shipped off, et cetera.
You didn't know what they thought.
You wondered what are they standing around for?
Are they really gloating that now we'll get rid of those Jews or whether they are just to see who is being taken away and what's happening.
You really didn't know.
Most people thought that they were gloating, you know.
But that may not be really correct, see?
So there we stood.
And all-- then, all of a sudden, a few people showed up.
One officer, Oberzahlmeister Frankfurter, which is a warrant officer-- chief warrant officer-- from the air force showed up.
He wanted his Jews that worked for air force.
He wanted to take them to the barracks at the air force.
So they called out the names of all these guys.
They put them aside.
And he marched off with them.
Then somebody came from the refinery.
There was a refinery in our town because there were oil wells in our area.
And he took his Jews.
Then came people from other establishments that they allowed them to take those Jews.
Then they called out names.
Now they called our names out.
Now, we could've taken our mother and our little brother.
Of course, whole families were left.
But we didn't know that, you see?
So they put us aside-- the electricians in town, us, the painters, certain that they need it for the economy, and put us aside.
The rest, the elderly, some of the elderly, the sick, and a number of trucks arrived.
And they forced them on those trucks.
There was a horrible commotion as young people, you know, wanted to go with their parents.
They wouldn't let their-- they didn't want their parents to go.
And the Gestapo would whip them back away from the trucks not to go.
But I remember one in particular whom I knew-- a very pretty girl about your age-- almost-- she looked about the same size-- wanted to go with her mother who was in a wheelchair.
And they forced the mother on the truck.
And she wouldn't let go.
And he whipped her.
Finally, he just pushed her on the truck and pushed her on there.
Within minutes, within minutes, two trucks with SA men, you would say SA, that have the swastika.
You know the green uniform, swastikas, came by with shovels, you know.
And they stood aside on the road.
And then a few jeeps with machine guns.
And I knew immediately that these people are doomed.
And they-- one of the Gestapo men went over to the road and showed them to start moving.
And these followed with the victims.
We found out the following morning that they were all executed and killed in-- in a forest preserve in Brzozow which was a little town not far from our town.
I then found out because there was a German who was an SA man.
And he wore-- he was a dispatcher of all trucks.
And he was our neighbor.
And he had taken liking to me all the time.
And he was a homosexual.
And he would accost me, not infrequently, you know.
He would come to our house and tell my mother he needed me because there was going to be an air raid.
I ought to come over to his apartment, you know, and so on.
And he was a dispatcher of the trucks.
So the following day, the maid-- his maid, who was a maid that then worked for the Gestapo, you see, told me the story.
There was a Jewish hat maker that lived also in our house below him.
He lived on top that German.
Paes was his name-- P-A-E-S.
And other guy-- the hat maker lived below him.
And he was telling that-- his maid how the hat maker was shot-- how he wrapped himself around in this prayer shawl, you know.
And he was praying as he ran into the mass grave.
And he was laughing his head off at that Paes, you know.
So she described that.
She told me that.
You know.
Anyway, they were killed.
We came back to the ghetto.
And in the meantime, the Gestapo went through the ghetto.
Fortunately, they didn't catch my mother and my family.
They'd catch anybody.
But they shot a number of people whom they called that did not go out to the cattle place.
Now the ghetto was reduced to just about,   I believe, 180 people.
See.
So when we came back, we let our mother and my little brother out of hiding.
About two weeks later-- oh, and we got when we were at the cattle place, we got a certificate-- you know, the blue card as you saw in you know, that we are allowed to stay.
So we had blue cards.
But my mother and my sister and my little brother didn't have a blue card.
See?
About a few weeks later, they announced that those that did not show up the first time-- if they will come out, they will get the blue card.
So a few people, very few, because no one trusted them, showed up, and believe it or not, they let them go and gave them the blue card.
Then a few weeks later, I happened to be assigned to work at a refinery near our town that day.
And when I came back in the evening, my mother and my sister were gone.
And I asked my father, what happened?
My father was crying bitterly.
Apparently they announced that whoever is going to show up is going to get the blue card.
And he let them out.
And of course, I found out from this German Paes that they were all killed the following day.
They were killed.
They were taken away.
And they were killed.
I found that out not only from him, because when I went to him, I wanted to find out whether he could help me out.
And he said, it's too late.
He says, why didn't you come to me earlier?
I would've told you that they ordered trucks-- that they were going to take them away.
And then I also found out my father was crying.
He was screaming get me because why am I pouring salt in his wounds because I was yelling at him, how come you let them go without me knowing?
I would've inquired first, you know, to find out what's going to be.
Well, anyway, that same day, my cousin's, my half cousins who worked for us, his wife comes back from that transport.
And she told the story what happened.
They loaded them on the trucks.
And as they were transporting them, a flat tire-- they got a flat tire on that truck-- on one of the few tucks that my mother and my sister were on, and she was on.
So had them get off.
And they stood by the side.
My mother went over to Schmatsler and told him, Herr Schmatsler, you know, I am the glasermeister's wife.
She described that story.
So he hit her in the face and told her to get back in line.
Now, whether he did it because other Gestapo were there, you know, I have a feeling that he probably would have let her go if there weren't others there.
But she and a number of peasants were coming and going towards our town.
So this cousin's wife of mine hooked herself on to those peasants and came back.
And this is how we found out what happened with that transport.
Then came the fateful day December 4, 1942-- the night before it was announced that the ghetto is now going to be completely liquidated.
And I have to tell you something else.
My younger brother, one year younger, they needed to some Jews to clean up after a ghetto that was liquidated, the Jaslo, where the headquarters of the Gestapo was.
That town didn't get a ghetto.
They were just all shipped straight out to the gas chambers.

By the way, I didn't mention to you that the majority of the town-- the majority of the Jews in town when we were on the capital market, were then taken to the station and shipped off to Belzec to the gas chambers.
And so when my Uncle Abraham with his children and-- and practically about 80% of all Jews.
And this is what-- when I talk to some of the churches or schools and I tell them, you know, that most people think that the Jews died in concentration camps.
They died long before that. 80% of our town was gone that July, August.
That was it.
They all went straight there and were killed the same day.
Now, they came December 4, and they said now the ghetto is going to be liquidated.
So-- the day before.
So now we knew this is it.
So my father decided that we better go and hide.
So I, my father-- not my younger brother.
He was shipped to Jaslo to clean up from that-- from the liquidation of the Jews there.
So he was there together with about 15 other Jews from our town to clean up there.

And so my father, I, my little brother, my-- my cousin Moshe, and his wife, the one who escaped from the transport, and their two little blonde children, [INAUDIBLE] about three years old-- two, three years old, we went out of the ghetto.
And in the store where we had, there was a basement where you lifted off the cover.
And there was a ladder you went into the basement.
The head of that store now was a Volksdeutsche, which is a Pole of German origin.
You see, there were Reichsdeutscher, which were the real Germans.
And there were the Volksdeutsche, which were the adopted Germans.
He was a Volksdeutsche.
And he wasn't there, because we were-- we used to work in that store.
And we went down in the basement.
And mind you, it's December.
It's very, very cold.
The little children-- the two children-- we had to hold their mouth so they wanted to cry so no one hears and gives us a way.
You see?
So we were hidden there all night.
Then early morning, Moshe takes a few plates of glass.
He goes out to look and see what goes on around the ghetto.
He goes out.
He comes right back.
And he says, the ghetto is ringed with Ukrainian guardsmen and machine guns.
Now, what do you do?
Where do you go?
We didn't know what to do.
We thought, well, we'll wait.
We waited.
And as it got a little lighter, that Volksdeutsche, the German, opens the store.
We crawl out of that basement.
And he takes a look at us and says, what are you doing here?
You can't be here.
The ghetto is being liquidated.
You can't be here.
Well, we told him we didn't know what to do.
We didn't know what's going to be.
So he said, you stay here.
I'm going to find out what goes on.
He goes out.
And he comes back with this fellow Becker, the Gestapo man.
And Becker says, take your stuff and come with me into the ghetto.
So we went in the ghetto.
And then he says, go into your apartment, take out some stuff, and line up here.
In the ghetto on that little street, there were already all Jews out there with a pack-- all of them there.
There were a few dead people already lying there that were shot.
You know?
We went in.
I went in the house.
And I happened to have a goose that I had gotten from a gentile, because this was-- this was a Thursday.
And-- no, the thing was on a Friday.
But on a Thursday, I got the goose.
And I had the ritual slaughterer slaughter it the day before.
And so I took the goose and shoved it under the bed there.
I-- we took into a pillowcase a few little items.
And we went out to the place.
And there we were.
And were still there-- my little brother-- my cousin and his wife with the two babies and my father.
And David, my first cousin who was a tool and die maker and his little boy-- his wife had been shot and was killed together with my mother.
You see?
So he was alone with this little boy.
He also worked for the Germans, because he was a master tool and die maker.
So he was fixing their locks and all kinds of such things, and so on.
Then there was a whole bunch of people being shot as they come out.
You see, as they come down, they are shot.
This one is shot.
Then the whole family comes out, the [?
Teppa ?] family, and they are put against the wall.
And they are shot.
And we are standing there.
Soon names are being called out again.
So they called my name out, my father, and my cousin out to go on the side.
The electrician, Mr.
Schachner was an electrician in our town.
He worked for the Gestapo too-- for the Germans.
And they put him aside and so on.
So we stood there.
All the other Jews where to be marched out.
Now, the dead came in a few horse wagons from the sanitary department.
And they just threw all the dead there.
And they took them out.
And the rest are marched out to the train station.
My little brother started crying.
Of course, he wanted to go with us.
And the Gestapo says, no.
So I went over to Schmatsler.
And I asked him to let my little brother with us.
And he said, no, I can't do it.
He's too young.
But he said, I promise you that he's not going to go [NON-ENGLISH].
That means he's not going to be killed.
They are going to Reichshof, which was a town not far from our town where there was a bigger ghetto.
They're going to go to that bigger ghetto.
My cousin, David, the tool and die maker, had his little boy.
He could've stayed.
But he didn't want his little boy to go.
So now the question was, what should we do?
Should we just go with transport and not stay?
You don't dare, you know, tell them that you don't want to stay and so on.
So we decided that David said he's going to take his boy.
And he's going to take my little brother.
The sight is very difficult to describe to you.
It's awful.
But anyway, they were all taken away and my cousin's wife with her little babies also.
And as we stood there, a few minutes later a Gestapo man-- still-- his name was Hauch.
He's from Jaslo.
He wasn't from our town.
He brings my cousin's wife back and the two babies and puts her on the side and shoots her and shoots the  two babies right then and there.
My cousin wants to run out.
So I grabbed him and held him back.
I tell him, look, is not your wife.
It's somebody else, and so on.
I'm sure he knew it was his wife.
I wouldn't let them go out there, because he's going to get killed.
